MOSS-TTS vs Chatterbox 8-bit

Measured on this M4 (24 GB), July 7 2026 · engines idle during measurement · same passage, same Brick Scott reference for all cloned rows

“Brother Francis Gerard of Utah might never have discovered the blessed documents, had it not been for the pilgrim with girded loins who appeared during that young novice’s Lenten fast in the desert…”

Chatterbox Original 8-bit Current best

Current premium engine — mlx-community/chatterbox-8bit

Faster than realtime. A 10 h book preps in ~5.4 h.

MOSS-TTS-Nano-100M Challenger

mlx-community/MOSS-TTS-Nano-100M — cloned, 48 kHz native

2–3× faster than Chatterbox with cloning. A 10 h book preps in ~2 h. Runs comfortably beside everything else.

MOSS-TTS-8B 8-bit — cached reference

Same 8B, Brick Scott reference encoded once and reused

Voice
Brick Scott (cloned)
RTF (M4, measured)
3.5–4.7
Model size

4× faster than the naive cloning path. Overnight shape: an 8 h idle window renders ~2 h of narration — can stay ahead of a one-book listener, but only just.

MOSS-TTS-8B 8-bit — cloned, naive

26× slower than Chatterbox. A 10 h book would take ~6 days.

MOSS-TTS-Nano-100M — long-form stability 3:45 continuous

v4 recipe: three ~1200-char chunks with a chars-per-second acceptance gate (all passed first try). Chunk joins are at ~1:15 and ~2:40 — listen there for voice consistency.

Voice
Brick Scott (cloned)
RTF (M4, measured)
0.55 at 1200-char chunks (0.21 at 280-char)
Model size
0.4 GB

Bigger chunks trade speed for far fewer voice-switch points: RTF matches Chatterbox (0.55 vs 0.54) but with only 2 joins in 3:35 instead of 13, better voice, 48 kHz, and 6× less memory.

Verdict, revised

The 8B is a maybe; the Nano is the story. With the reference encoded once and cached, the 8B clones at RTF ~4 — workable only as an “overnight studio” tier that stays a couple of hours ahead of a single active book, while pinning 9.8 GB of unified memory.

MOSS-TTS-Nano-100M clones Brick Scott at RTF 0.17–0.24 in ~0.4 GB — two to three times faster than the current Chatterbox engine, at 48 kHz. If the listening quality holds up against Chatterbox (judge with the players above), it is a direct candidate to replace or sit beside Chatterbox as the premium engine, and it is small enough to coexist with everything else. Next steps if it passes the ear test: longer-passage stability check, then a drop-in engine server on the chatterbox HTTP contract.
